skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation skill for AI agents using inference.sh and Playwright-style sessions. It can navigate pages, interact with elements, capture screenshots or videos, execute page JavaScript, upload files, use proxies, and support authenticated workflows.
skill.summary: Automate browser sessions with element refs, screenshots, videos, JavaScript execution, proxies, and reusable sessions.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, network, filesystem, and sensitive-data patterns. Most shell detections are expected documentation or template usage, but the skill confirms high-risk browser capabilities: page JavaScript execution, authenticated cookie access, screenshots, videos, file upload, and proxy routing. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than blocked.

high_findings.title: Proxy Rotation Guidance for Rate Limit Avoidance
high_findings.description: The proxy reference explicitly describes rotating proxies for web scraping and rate limit avoidance. This is abuse-enabling guidance because it can help bypass service controls and attribution safeguards.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant section is labeled rate limit avoidance and shows proxy rotation logic. The behavior is explicit, not inferred from a weak static pattern.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Cookie and Page Data Extraction
high_findings.description: The authentication guide includes cookie extraction through page JavaScript, and the command reference supports arbitrary code execution in the page context. This can expose session cookies, tokens, page content, and sensitive authenticated data.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly document document.cookie access and page JavaScript execution. Legitimate testing use is possible, but the sensitive-data exposure risk is clear.

high_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability Can Exfiltrate Files
high_findings.description: The skill supports uploading local file paths to web file inputs. If an agent is induced to use sensitive paths, this can send local files to a remote site.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file upload capability is explicitly documented with file_paths. The risk depends on user authorization and target site, so this is high risk but not proof of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots and Videos May Capture Sensitive Sessions
medium_findings.description: The skill can capture screenshots and record complete browser sessions. The documentation warns against recording sensitive sessions, but the capability can still expose credentials, personal data, or internal application screens.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Video and screenshot capture are core documented capabilities. The presence of a best-practice warning lowers concern but does not remove the data exposure risk.

medium_findings.title: Executable Shell Templates Run Networked Browser Commands
medium_findings.description: The bundled shell templates invoke the external browser service and process user-provided URLs or workflow data. They appear intended for legitimate automation, but they require command execution and network access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The templates are real bash scripts, not only markdown examples. I did not find shell eval or clear command injection, so this is moderate operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Authenticated Sessions Require Careful Cleanup
medium_findings.description: The authentication guide encourages reusable authenticated sessions and notes that sessions maintain cookies. Persistent sessions increase exposure if not closed or if shared across tasks without isolation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The session reuse behavior is explicit and expected for this skill. The risk is contextual, so it is not classified as malicious.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL detections point to example domains, installation links, and sample targets in documentation. These do not show hidden callbacks or unauthorized exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are visible examples or documentation links. I found no evidence that the skill silently calls a hardcoded exfiltration endpoint.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Heuristics Are Relative Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal detections are caused by relative markdown links such as references back to SKILL.md. I did not find evidence that user-controlled paths are joined to traverse directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequences appear in markdown links, not path access code. File upload remains separately covered as a capability risk.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the reviewed context shows browser documentation, tables, and examples rather than cryptographic code. No evidence found for real cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I checked representative cited areas and did not find hashing or encryption logic. This appears to be a heuristic false positive from markdown content.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Browser Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Instructions and templates repeatedly invoke belt or infsh to start remote browser automation sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external command pattern is explicit and central to the skill. It is expected behavior but increases marketplace risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: In-Page JavaScript Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function runs caller-provided JavaScript in the active page context and returns results plus a screenshot.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code execution capability is directly documented. It can be legitimate for testing but can also extract sensitive page data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy Routing Including SOCKS5
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill supports proxy_url, proxy credentials, and SOCKS5 proxy routing for browser traffic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Proxy support is explicit in function options and examples. The risk is heightened by separate rate limit avoidance guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screen and Video Capture
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill captures screenshots and can record all browser activity throughout a session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Capture features are explicitly documented and include potentially sensitive browser content. The docs include some safety guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Path Uploads
dangerous_patterns.description: The interact action accepts file_paths for uploading local files to web file inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file upload input is explicitly documented. Misuse requires unsafe user or agent choices, so it is a capability risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

content.value_statement: AI agents often need reliable browser actions without parsing full page markup. This skill provides session-based navigation, element refs, screenshots, videos, and page interaction commands.
actual_capabilities: Opens browser sessions against specified URLs and returns compact element refs.
Clicks, fills, types, selects, scrolls, waits, drags, and uploads files through element refs.
Captures viewport or full-page screenshots during browser workflows.
Records browser sessions as video when recording is enabled at session start.
Runs JavaScript in the page context for extraction or interaction tasks.
Supports authenticated sessions, proxy configuration, and reusable shell workflow templates.
limitations: Requires the external inference.sh CLI and an authenticated account.
Element refs become stale after navigation or major page changes.
Video recording is session-level and only returned when the session closes.
The skill is dual-use and requires strict safeguards for credentials, cookies, and file uploads.

use_cases.title: Verify User Flows
use_cases.description: Run browser steps, capture screenshots, and record failures for web application testing.

use_cases.title: Collect Public Page Evidence
use_cases.description: Open public pages, extract visible content, save links, and preserve screenshots for review.

use_cases.title: Document Product Workflows
use_cases.description: Record guided browser sessions that show settings changes, onboarding flows, or support steps.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Summarize a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the target URL, list the visible interactive elements, summarize the page purpose, and close the session.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Evidence for a Web Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this URL, capture a full-page screenshot, extract visible page text, save the main links, and close the session.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the form page, identify field refs, fill the provided test values, submit the form, verify the result, and capture a screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Debug an Authenticated Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a new browser session with recording disabled, complete the login steps using provided credentials, verify authentication, perform the requested task, and close the session.

output_examples.input: Inspect a public landing page and identify clickable elements.
output_examples.output: A short page summary, the current URL and title, key element refs, and a screenshot reference.

output_examples.input: Submit a test contact form with provided non-sensitive data.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step action summary, final page state, success or failure signal, and captured evidence.

output_examples.input: Record a product settings walkthrough for documentation.
output_examples.output: A completed browser session summary with a returned video artifact and notes about each major action.

best_practices: Use only authorized sites, accounts, and data sources for browser automation.
Disable video and screenshots when pages may contain secrets or personal data.
Re-snapshot after navigation or dynamic page changes before using element refs again.
anti_patterns: Do not use proxy rotation to evade rate limits or access controls.
Do not extract cookies, session tokens, or credentials unless explicitly authorized.
Do not upload local files unless the target, file path, and user consent are verified.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, element interaction, screenshots, video recording, JavaScript execution, proxy setup, and session workflows.

faq.question: Does it require another tool?
faq.answer: Yes. The instructions depend on the inference.sh CLI and authenticated browser sessions.

faq.question: Can it handle login flows?
faq.answer: Yes, but credentials and session data must be handled carefully and only for authorized accounts.

faq.question: Can it scrape websites?
faq.answer: It can extract visible text and links, but users must respect site terms, robots policies, and rate limits.

faq.question: Why is this skill high risk?
faq.answer: It can execute page JavaScript, capture screens, upload files, use proxies, and interact with authenticated sessions.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish without review?
faq.answer: No. It should receive human review and marketplace warnings before publication.
